questions.text: A vector is a physical quantity which has
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option describes a scalar, which only has magnitude without any direction, thus not defining a vector.
A vector is characterized by having both magnitude and direction. This is the complete definition that separates vectors from scalars.
While direction is an aspect of a vector, it is incomplete without magnitude. A vector must include both elements.
This is incorrect because a vector cannot be defined by just magnitude or just direction; both are essential components.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that a vector has both magnitude and direction. This is what fundamentally distinguishes vectors from scalars, which possess only magnitude. Option A is incorrect because it describes a scalar, lacking the directional component necessary for a vector. Option C is incorrect because simply having direction without magnitude does not fully characterize a vector. Option D is incorrect as vectors require both magnitude and direction, not merely one of these elements.

questions.text: A rectangular component of 5N force is 4N. The other component is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect. The components of a force do not add up linearly; they are vector components.
Correct. Using the Pythagorean theorem, the other component is 3N.
Incorrect. This value does not satisfy the Pythagorean relationship for the given force.
Incorrect. This value is too large; remember to calculate using the Pythagorean theorem.

questions.explanation: To find the other component of a force when one component and the resultant force are known, use the Pythagorean theorem. For a force of 5N with a known component of 4N, the other component can be calculated as follows: \( \sqrt{5^2 - 4^2} = \sqrt{25 - 16} = \sqrt{9} = 3 \). Thus, the other component is 3N. Option A is incorrect because it neglects the vector nature of force components. Option C is incorrect as it does not satisfy the Pythagorean theorem. Option D is incorrect as it overestimates the component size.

questions.text: A vector in space has
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A vector requires more than one component to fully describe its direction and magnitude in space, as space is multidimensional.
In three-dimensional space, a vector is defined by three components: x, y, and z. These components describe the vector's direction and magnitude across the three axes.
Two components describe a vector in a two-dimensional plane, not in three-dimensional space where three components are needed.
This option is incorrect because vectors in three-dimensional space are specifically described by three components.

questions.explanation: A vector in three-dimensional space is characterized by three components, each aligned with one of the spatial dimensions: x, y, and z. This allows the vector to describe direction and magnitude within the full scope of three-dimensional space. Thus, Option B is correct. Option A is incorrect because a single component cannot capture the multi-directional nature of space. Option C is incorrect, as it refers to two-dimensional vectors, which do not account for the z-axis. Option D is incorrect because it denies the established definition of spatial vectors having three components.

questions.text: A vector on a plane has
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A vector in a plane requires more than one component as it needs to represent both magnitude and direction across two dimensions.
A vector on a plane has two components corresponding to the x and y axes, which are needed to fully describe its magnitude and direction in two-dimensional space.
Three components are necessary for describing a vector in three-dimensional space, which is beyond the scope of a two-dimensional plane.
This is incorrect, as a vector on a plane is accurately described by two components.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: A vector on a plane has two components. A plane is a two-dimensional surface, and a vector within this plane can be decomposed into two perpendicular components, usually aligned with the x and y axes. Option A is incorrect because a single component cannot adequately describe both the magnitude and direction in the plane. Option C is incorrect because three components are used to describe vectors in three-dimensional space, not on a two-dimensional plane. Option D is incorrect as the correct number of components for a vector on a plane is two.

questions.text: The cross product i x j is equal to
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The cross product of two non-parallel vectors is not zero; it results in a vector perpendicular to both.
The cross product yields a vector, not a scalar quantity like 1.
This would be the result of the cross product j x i, which is the reverse order of i x j.
The cross product i x j results in the unit vector k, pointing in the positive z-axis direction according to the right-hand rule.

questions.explanation: The cross product of two vectors results in a third vector that is orthogonal to the plane formed by the original vectors. For the standard unit vectors, i, j, and k in the Cartesian coordinate system, the right-hand rule helps determine the direction of the resulting vector. When you perform the cross product i x j, point your index finger in the direction of i (x-axis) and your middle finger in the direction of j (y-axis); your thumb will then point in the direction of k (z-axis). Therefore, the cross product i x j equals k.Option A (Zero) is incorrect because the cross product of two perpendicular vectors is never zero. Option B (1) is incorrect because the cross product results in a vector, not a scalar. Option C (-k) is incorrect because the negative sign would indicate the opposite direction, which corresponds to j x i, not i x j.

questions.text: The angle between the vector A=2i+3j +6 k and B = 31+ 2j - 2k is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The vectors are perpendicular because their dot product equals zero, leading to an angle of 90°.
An angle of 0° would imply the vectors are parallel and have a non-zero dot product, contrary to the zero dot product observed here.
A 45° angle suggests partial alignment, but since the dot product is zero, the vectors are actually perpendicular.
An angle of 180° indicates that vectors are in opposite directions, which would result in a negative dot product, not zero.

questions.explanation: To find the angle between vectors A and B, use the dot product formula: A · B = (2i + 3j + 6k) · (3i + 2j - 2k) = 2*3 + 3*2 + 6*(-2) = 0. A dot product of zero indicates that the vectors are perpendicular, hence the angle between them is 90°. Other options are incorrect: 0° implies parallel vectors with a non-zero dot product; 45° suggests partial alignment, inconsistent with a zero dot product; and 180° implies opposite directions with a negative dot product, which does not apply here.

questions.text: A force F=2i+3j units, has its point of application moved from the point (1, 3) to the point (5, 7). Work done is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Correct! The work done is calculated by finding the dot product of the force vector with the displacement vector, resulting in 20J.
Incorrect. Ensure that you have correctly calculated the displacement vector before finding the dot product with the force vector.
Incorrect. Double-check your calculation of the dot product between the force vector and the displacement vector.
Incorrect. This value is too high. Review your calculations of the displacement and the dot product.

questions.explanation: To determine the work done, calculate the displacement vector by subtracting the initial position from the final position, giving (5-1)i + (7-3)j = 4i + 4j. Then, find the dot product with the force vector F = 2i + 3j:Work = (2i + 3j) • (4i + 4j) = 2*4 + 3*4 = 8 + 12 = 20J.Therefore, the correct answer is 20J. The other options are incorrect due to errors in calculating either the displacement vector or the dot product.

questions.text: All of the following are scalars except
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Density is a scalar because it is defined as mass per unit volume, having only magnitude with no directional component.
Magnetic flux density, also known as B-field, is a vector quantity as it not only has magnitude but also a direction, which is the direction of the magnetic field lines.
Electromotive force (E.M.F) refers to the energy provided per charge by an energy source, and it is a scalar quantity as it possesses only magnitude.
Voltage is a measure of electric potential difference and is a scalar quantity because it only has magnitude.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Magnetic flux density because it is a vector quantity, characterized by both magnitude and direction. This is distinct from the other options—density, E.M.F, and voltage—which are scalar quantities, having magnitude only without any directional attribute.

questions.text: When a vector is multiplied by a negative number its direction
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect because multiplying a vector by a negative number reverses its direction, so it cannot remain the same.
This is correct because multiplying a vector by a negative number results in a direction reversal, equivalent to a 180° change.
This is incorrect because the direction of a vector does change when it is multiplied by a negative number, reversing its orientation.
This is incorrect because a 210° change does not accurately represent the effect of multiplying a vector by a negative number, which is a 180° change.

questions.explanation: When a vector is multiplied by a negative number, its direction is reversed, which is equivalent to a 180° rotation from its original direction. Thus, option B is the correct answer. Option A and Option C are incorrect because they suggest no change in direction, which contradicts the effect of multiplication by a negative number. Option D is incorrect because a 210° change is not the result of such multiplication.

The correct form is r = xi + yj, where x and y are the respective coordinates of the point. Other options incorrectly incorporate the z-component, which is irrelevant in the xy-plane context.

questions.text: Two forces each of magnitude F act perpendicular to each other, the angle made by the resultantforce with the horizontal will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the forces were not equal, the angle could be different. Here, the forces are equal.
When two equal forces act at a right angle, the resultant force makes a 45-degree angle with each force.
A 60-degree angle could occur in different vector scenarios, but not when equal forces are perpendicular.
A 90-degree angle would occur if the resultant was aligned with one of the force vectors, which is not the case here.

questions.explanation: When two forces of equal magnitude act perpendicularly, they form a right-angled triangle with the resultant force as the hypotenuse. The angle formed by the resultant force with each of the original forces is 45 degrees, because the triangle is isosceles with the equal sides being the two forces. Option B (45 degrees) is correct. Other options are incorrect as they don't represent the angle formed in this specific scenario.

questions.text: The angle between two rectangular components of any vector is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An angle of 30 degrees is not typical for rectangular components, which are perpendicular to each other.
An angle of 50 degrees does not align with the definition of rectangular components, which are always at right angles.
180 degrees represents a straight line, not the perpendicular nature of rectangular components.
Rectangular components are defined to be perpendicular to each other, which means they form a 90-degree angle.

questions.explanation: Rectangular components of a vector are two vectors at right angles to each other. Therefore, the angle between them is always 90 degrees. This is because the term 'rectangular' implies a right angle, forming the basis for the Cartesian coordinate system. Options 30, 50, and 180 degrees do not correspond to the perpendicular nature of rectangular components.

questions.text: A force of 5 N is acting along Y-axis. Its components along x-axis is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that a component exists along the x-axis, which is incorrect as the force is purely along the Y-axis.
Correct! Since the force is entirely along the Y-axis, it has no component along the x-axis.
This option incorrectly assumes a larger magnitude along the x-axis, which is not possible as the force is only along the Y-axis.
This option repeats the misconception that the force has a component along the x-axis, which it does not.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is zero because the force is acting purely along the Y-axis. In vector decomposition, a force acting along one axis does not have any component along the perpendicular axis. Therefore, the x-component of a force solely along the Y-axis is zero. The other options incorrectly suggest a non-zero component along the x-axis, which is not possible here.

questions.text: If the resultant of two vectors each of magnitudes F is also of magnitude F, the angle between the will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the angle is 90 degrees, the resultant magnitude would be greater than the magnitude of each vector.
If the angle is 60 degrees, the resultant magnitude would be greater than the magnitude of each vector.
If the angle is 30 degrees, the resultant magnitude would be greater than the magnitude of each vector.
When the angle between two equal vectors is 120 degrees, the resultant magnitude is equal to the magnitude of each vector.

questions.explanation: To find the angle between two vectors of equal magnitude F whose resultant is also of magnitude F, use the formula for the resultant of two vectors: R = √(A² + B² + 2ABcosθ). Here, A and B are the magnitudes of the vectors, and θ is the angle between them. Substituting the values, we get:F = √(F² + F² + 2F²cosθ)Squaring both sides gives:F² = 2F²(1 + cosθ)Dividing throughout by F²:1 = 2(1 + cosθ)1 = 2 + 2cosθ2cosθ = -1cosθ = -1/2Therefore, θ = 120 degrees. Thus, the correct answer is 120 degrees. The other options yield angles where the resultant would have a different magnitude than F.

questions.text: A force vector F of magnitude 30N, making an angle of 60 ^ 0 with x -axis, its x component will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This value is incorrect because it does not correctly use the cosine of 60 degrees.
This is the correct value obtained by calculating 30N × cos(60°).
This value is incorrect and doesn't result from applying the cosine function correctly.
This value results from an incorrect calculation of the cosine component.

questions.explanation: To find the x-component of the force vector F, we use the formula: F_x = F × cos(θ), where F is the magnitude of the force and θ is the angle with the x-axis. Here, F = 30N and θ = 60°. Therefore, F_x = 30N × cos(60°) = 30N × 0.5 = 15N. The correct answer is 15N. The other options are incorrect because they result from either not using the cosine function or miscalculating the trigonometric component.

questions.text: The resultant of two forces of 3N and 4N making an angle 90 with each other is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests an incorrect calculation. The forces are perpendicular, so the Pythagorean theorem should be used.
This option suggests a simple addition of magnitudes, which is incorrect for perpendicular vectors. Use the Pythagorean theorem instead.
Correct! By using the Pythagorean theorem, the resultant of two perpendicular forces of 3N and 4N is 5N.
This option results from incorrect calculation. The forces are at a 90-degree angle, so calculate using the Pythagorean theorem.

questions.explanation: To find the resultant of two forces that are perpendicular to each other, you should use the Pythagorean theorem. Here, the forces are 3N and 4N, making an angle of 90 degrees with each other. The formula for the resultant R is R = √(3² + 4²) = √(9 + 16) = √25 = 5N. Therefore, the correct answer is 5N. The other options are incorrect because they either involve incorrect calculations or misunderstand the need to apply the Pythagorean theorem to perpendicular forces.

questions.text: Two force act together on an object. The magnitude of their resultant is minimum when the an between the forces is.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When two forces act at an angle of 0 degrees, they are in the same direction, resulting in maximum resultant force.
An angle of 45 degrees between two forces does not yield the minimum resultant; it results in a reduced but not minimal resultant.
While an angle of 60 degrees reduces the resultant compared to 0 degrees, it does not minimize it.
When two forces act at an angle of 180 degrees, they are in opposite directions, leading to a minimum resultant equal to the difference in their magnitudes.

questions.explanation: The magnitude of the resultant force is minimized when two forces are acting in opposite directions, which corresponds to an angle of 180 degrees between them. This is because the forces subtract from each other, resulting in the smallest possible resultant, which is equal to the difference in their magnitudes. In contrast, when the forces act at an angle of 0 degrees, they are in the same direction, resulting in the maximum possible resultant. Angles of 45 or 60 degrees result in reduced resultants compared to 0 degrees, but they are not the minimum.

questions.text: If the resultant of two vectors, each of magnitudes F, is twice of magnitude F, the angle betw them will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: At 90°, the resultant magnitude is √2 times the magnitude of each vector, not twice.
At 60°, the resultant magnitude is √3 times the magnitude of each vector, not twice.
At 0°, the vectors are aligned, and the resultant is simply 2F, which matches the condition given.
At 120°, the resultant magnitude is equal to the magnitude of one of the vectors, not twice.

questions.explanation: For the resultant of two vectors to be twice the magnitude of each individual vector, the vectors must be in the same direction, which means the angle between them is 0 degrees. When the angle is 0, the vectors add directly to give a resultant of 2F. At 90°, the resultant is √2F, at 60° it is √3F, and at 120° it becomes F. Hence, the correct answer is 0°.

questions.text: If A = 3i + 4j then magnitude will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This answer is incorrect. It mistakenly adds the components instead of using the Pythagorean theorem.
This answer is incorrect. It results from squaring the components and adding them but forgetting to take the square root.
This is the correct answer. The magnitude is calculated using the formula \( \sqrt{(3)^2 + (4)^2} \), which equals 5.
This answer is incorrect. It may result from a misunderstanding or miscalculation.

questions.explanation: The magnitude of a vector \( A = 3i + 4j \) is found using the formula \( \sqrt{(3)^2 + (4)^2} \). Squaring the components gives 9 and 16, respectively. Adding these results in 25. The square root of 25 is 5. Therefore, the correct magnitude is 5. Other options are incorrect due to errors in calculation or misunderstanding of the magnitude formula.

questions.text: If two force each of magnitude 5N acts along the same line in same direction on a body, then magnitude of their resultant will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This result would imply that one of the forces was not considered.
This result would suggest an incorrect addition of forces.
Correct. When two forces of 5N each act in the same direction, their magnitudes add up to 10N.

questions.explanation: When two forces act along the same line in the same direction, their magnitudes simply add up. In this case, both forces have a magnitude of 5N, so the resultant force is 5N + 5N = 10N. Option A (5N) is incorrect because it does not account for both forces. Option B (30N) is incorrect because it suggests an incorrect summation of forces. Option D (20N) is incorrect as it assumes each force is greater than 5N.

questions.text: All the following are vectors except:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Impulse is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction, as it is the change in momentum.
Momentum is a vector quantity because it depends on both the mass and velocity of an object, and velocity is a vector.
Torque is a vector quantity because it results from a force applied at a distance, and it has both magnitude and direction.
Density is a scalar quantity because it is defined as mass per unit volume and has magnitude only, without direction.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Density. Density is a scalar quantity as it only has magnitude, defined as mass per unit volume, and does not have a direction. In contrast, Impulse, Momentum, and Torque are all vector quantities because they involve both magnitude and direction. Impulse is the change in momentum, momentum depends on velocity, and torque involves rotational movement, all of which require directionality.

This is correct. A unit vector is used to indicate the direction of a vector while having a magnitude of one.
This is incorrect. Position refers to a point in space, whereas a unit vector is used to indicate direction.

questions.explanation: A unit vector is primarily used to indicate the direction of a vector. It has a magnitude of one, which makes it a convenient tool to represent direction without affecting the vector's magnitude. Option C is correct because it accurately describes the purpose of a unit vector. Option A is incorrect because the magnitude of a unit vector is always one, not the magnitude of the vector it represents. Option B is incorrect because location is not described by a unit vector but rather by coordinates. Option D is incorrect because position refers to an absolute point in space, which is not what a unit vector specifies.

questions.text: Vector A = Axi + Ayj lies in
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In the first quadrant, both Ax and Ay are positive.
In the third quadrant, both Ax and Ay would be negative, which is incorrect for this case.
In the second quadrant, Ax would be negative while Ay would be positive, which is incorrect for this case.
In the fourth quadrant, Ax would be positive while Ay would be negative, which is incorrect for this case.

questions.explanation: Vector A = Axi + Ayj lies in the first quadrant because both Ax and Ay are positive in this quadrant. In other quadrants, the signs of Ax and Ay do not match the condition where both are positive. The third quadrant has both Ax and Ay negative, the second quadrant has a negative Ax and a positive Ay, and the fourth quadrant has a positive Ax and a negative Ay. Thus, only the first quadrant satisfies the condition of both components being positive.

questions.text: The angle of a vector A=Ax i-Ay j with the x-axis will be in between:

options.explanation: This range represents angles in the first quadrant where both x and y components are positive. Here, Ax is positive and Ay is negative, which doesn't fit this quadrant.
This range corresponds to the second quadrant where the x component is negative and the y component is positive. Since Ax is positive and Ay is negative, it doesn't fit this quadrant.

This places the vector in the fourth quadrant where angles are measured from 270° to 360°. In this quadrant, the x-axis is positive, and the y-axis is negative. Therefore, Option D is correct. The other options represent angles in quadrants where the sign of the components does not match the vector described.

questions.text: A vector having magnitude equal to given vector but in opposite direction is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A unit vector has a magnitude of 1 and is used to indicate direction, but is not necessarily opposite in direction to another vector.
A positive vector typically refers to a vector pointing in a conventional positive direction, not the opposite of a given vector.
A negative vector has the same magnitude as the original vector but points in the opposite direction.
A null vector has zero magnitude and no direction, differing from a vector that is simply in the opposite direction.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is a negative vector. When a vector is reversed in direction while maintaining the same magnitude, it becomes a negative of the original vector. This concept is crucial in vector algebra where the negative of a vector -A has the same magnitude as vector A but points in the opposite direction.The other options are incorrect because a unit vector is defined solely by its direction and has a magnitude of one, not related to the direction of another vector. A positive vector refers to a vector in a conventional positive direction, not one that is reversed. A null vector has no magnitude or direction, which does not apply to this context.

questions.text: The magnitude of resultant of three components of a vector is 3. Its x-component is 2.y-components is 1 then its z-component will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. It is calculated using the formula for the magnitude of a vector in three dimensions.
This option assumes that the z-component is equal to the y-component, which is incorrect when considering the given magnitude.
This option is too large and does not satisfy the given magnitude of the vector when calculated.
Assuming the z-component is zero ignores its contribution to the overall magnitude of the vector, which is incorrect.

questions.explanation: To find the z-component of the vector, use the formula for the magnitude of a vector in three dimensions: \( \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} = \text{magnitude} \). Given that the magnitude is 3, the x-component is 2, and the y-component is 1, substitute these values into the equation:\( \sqrt{2^2 + 1^2 + z^2} = 3 \)Squaring both sides gives:\( 4 + 1 + z^2 = 9 \)\( z^2 = 4 \)\( z = 2 \) or \( z = -2 \), but since we're looking for magnitude, we take the positive value, so the z-component is 2.The other options do not satisfy the equation for the magnitude of the vector given the components.

questions.text: Which pair of the following forces will never give resultant force of 2N?
questions.subject: Physics

The resultant can be 2N (if opposite), or range from 2N to 4N depending on the angle. This option repeats, see explanation.
The resultant force can either be 3N (if opposite) or 5N (if in the same direction), but never 2N.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 4N, 1N. This is because the minimum resultant force occurs when the two forces act in opposite directions, yielding a resultant of 3N (4N - 1N), and the maximum resultant is 5N (4N + 1N) when they act in the same direction. Therefore, 2N is not achievable.For the other options:With 2N, 2N, the resultant can range from 0N to 4N, including 2N.With 3N, 1N, the resultant can be exactly 2N when the forces are opposite each other.The repeated 3N, 1N option is covered by the same reasoning as the previous 3N, 1N option.

Since the given angle is 63 degrees, which is larger than 45 degrees, the y-component will indeed be the longer one. Option A 'No' is incorrect because there must be a longer component. Option B 'Both' is incorrect as both components cannot simultaneously be longer than each other. Option C 'X' is incorrect because the x-component is shorter than the y-component at an angle of 63 degrees with the x-axis.

questions.text: Two forces each of 10N magnitude acting on a body, if the forces are inclined at 30° and 60° with x-component of their resultant is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option represents a misunderstanding of the vector addition process or the decomposition of forces.
This option is likely a result of miscalculation or misunderstanding the unit of force components.
This is incorrect and possibly stems from a significant error in calculating the cosine components.
This is the correct calculation of the x-component of the resultant force when both forces are decomposed and summed.

questions.explanation: To find the x-component of the resultant force, decompose each force into its x-component using the cosine of the angle between the force and the x-axis. The first force has an x-component of 10N * cos(30°) and the second force has an x-component of 10N * cos(60°). Calculating these gives: 10 * (sqrt(3)/2) + 10 * (1/2) = 8.66N + 5N = 13.66N. Therefore, Option D is correct. Option A (10N) does not account for the angles; Option B (136.6N) is a computation error possibly mixing up force components; Option C (1.366N) involves a miscalculation of the cosine components.

questions.text: If the vector A and B are of magnitude 4 and 3 cm making of 30 ^ 9 and 90° respectively with x-axis their scalar product will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This would be the result if the vectors were perpendicular, but they are not.
This would be correct if the angle between the vectors was 0°, leading to a maximum dot product.
This is the correct answer as it uses the correct angle and formula for the scalar product.
This result does not account for the correct angle between the vectors.

questions.explanation: The scalar product (dot product) of two vectors A and B is given by the formula A · B = |A| |B| cos(θ), where |A| and |B| are the magnitudes of the vectors and θ is the angle between them. Here, |A| = 4 cm, |B| = 3 cm, and θ = 30°. Therefore, A · B = 4 * 3 * cos(30°) = 12 * (√3/2) = 6√3 cm2. The correct answer is 6.0 cm2. The other options are incorrect because they either assume the wrong angle or do not apply the formula correctly.

questions.text: IF A = Ai and B = Bj then A.B is equal to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This result would imply a scalar multiplication without considering the direction of the vectors.
The dot product of two perpendicular vectors is zero.
This would be the result if both vectors were unit vectors in the same direction.
This implies a vector result, which is incorrect for a dot product, as it gives a scalar.

questions.explanation: The dot product A.B of two vectors A = Ai and B = Bj involves multiplying their magnitudes and the cosine of the angle between them. Since i and j are unit vectors in perpendicular directions, the angle between them is 90 degrees, and thus, cos(90°) = 0. Therefore, the dot product A.B = A * B * cos(90°) = 0. This explains why the correct answer is zero. Option A suggests a simple multiplication of magnitudes, ignoring direction; Option C would imply identical unit vectors; Option D suggests a vector result, which is incorrect for dot products.

questions.text: If the dot product of two non-zero vectors vanishes, the vectors will be:

questions.explanation: To determine the angle between the vectors A = 2i + 3j - k and B = 4i + 6j - 2k, we use the dot product formula: A • B = |A| |B| cos(θ). The angle θ is found using the formula cos(θ) = (A • B) / (|A| |B|). Substituting the values, cos(θ) = 28 / (√14 * √56) = 1, indicating θ = 0°.This confirms that the vectors are parallel. questions.explanation: The given position vector P=(a cos ωt)i + (a sin ωt)j describes a circular motion with radius a. This velocity vector is perpendicular to the position vector at all times, as the dot product of the velocity and position vectors is zero. Therefore, the correct answer is that the velocity of the particle is perpendicular to the position vector.

questions.text: Two vectors A and B are such that |C|= |A| +|B|| If θ is the angle between direction of vector A and vector B then the correct statement is?

However, the correct calculation requires the magnitude to be squared, hence the correct unit vector is 1/49 (3i + 6j - 2k). questions.text: There are two force vectors, one of 5 N and other of 12 N at what angle the two vectors be added to get resultant vector of 17 N. 7 N and 13 N respectively

questions.explanation: To find the resultant vector of two forces, we use the vector addition formula: R = √(A² + B² + 2ABcosθ), where A and B are the magnitudes of the forces, and θ is the angle between them. When the angle is 0°, the vectors are in the same direction, and their magnitudes add up directly: 5 N + 12 N = 17 N. When the angle is 180°, the vectors are in opposite directions, and their magnitudes subtract: |5 N - 12 N| = 7 N. When the angle is 90°, the vectors are perpendicular, and we use the Pythagorean theorem: √(5² + 12²) = 13 N. Hence, the correct angles are 0°, 180°, and 90° for the resultant vectors 17 N, 7 N, and 13 N, respectively. questions.text: Two forces each of magnitude F have a resultant of the same magnitude F. The angle between the two forces is

questions.explanation: To find the angle between two forces of magnitude F that result in a resultant force of the same magnitude F, we can use the law of cosines: R2 = F2 + F2 + 2 * F * F * cos(θ) Given that the resultant R is also F, we substitute: F2 = 2F2 + 2F2 * cos(θ) Simplifying, we get: F2 = 2F2 * (1 + cos(θ)) Simplifying further: 1 = 2 * (1 + cos(θ)) Solving for cos(θ) gives: cos(θ) = -1/2 This corresponds to an angle of 120°. Therefore, the correct answer is 120°, as the forces form an equilateral triangle with the resultant when the angle between them is 120°. questions.text: If A + B= 7i^ +7k^ and A-B= -i^ + k^, then the magnitude of A will be:

To find its magnitude, we can use the formula: |A| = √(Ax^2 + Ay^2 + Az^2) In this case, Ax = 3, Ay = 0, and Az = 4. questions.text: If two equal forces act perpendicularly upon each other, then the angle between the resultant force and a horizontal surface will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the two forces are equal and act perpendicularly to each other, the resultant force cannot be zero degrees unless one of the forces is zero. This option is incorrect.
A 30-degree angle would only be possible if the two forces have different magnitudes. Since the question states that the forces are equal, this option is incorrect.

When two equal forces are applied perpendicularly to each other, they create a right angle. In such a case, the resultant force can be determined using the Pythagorean theorem. The magnitude of the resultant force is equal to the square root of the sum of the squares of the magnitudes of the two forces.Since the two forces are equal, their magnitudes are the same. Therefore, if we consider each force to have a magnitude of "F," the magnitude of the resultant force will be:Resultant force = √(F^2 + F^2) = √(2F^2) = √2FNow, when the resultant force is resolved into horizontal and vertical components, each component will have a magnitude of √2F. Since the vertical component is equal to the force acting vertically, the angle between the resultant force and the horizontal surface will be 45 degrees.Therefore, the correct option is C, 45 degrees.
A 60-degree angle would only be possible if the two forces have different magnitudes. Since the question states that the forces are equal, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: When two equal forces act perpendicularly, they form a right triangle with the resultant force as the hypotenuse. Using the Pythagorean theorem, if each force has a magnitude 'F', the resultant force is: √(F² + F²) = √2F.The angle θ between the resultant force and the horizontal is calculated using trigonometry: tan(θ) = opposite/adjacent = F/F = 1, giving θ = 45°. Therefore, the correct answer is 45°.Other options imply unequal force magnitudes or incorrect geometric interpretations, which do not apply here.

The vectors F₁ and F₂ are arranged head-to-tail.However, F₃ is also pointing outward rather than completing the triangle.The triangle does not close, and the resultant of F₁ + F₂ is not balanced by F₃.Again, this setup does not reflect zero net force.Conclusion: Incorrect

F₁ and F₂ are placed head-to-tail, but F₃ is pointing in the same general direction as the others.It fails to act as the opposing force to the resultant of F₁ + F₂.Therefore, it doesn’t complete the vector triangle properly.No equilibrium is achieved here either.Conclusion: Incorrect

This is the correct vector representation.F₁ and F₂ are placed head-to-tail, and then F₃ starts from the head of F₂ and ends at the tail of F₁, forming a closed triangle.This means:F₁ + F₂ + F₃ = 0, indicating the body is in perfect equilibrium.The frictional force F₃ is correctly shown as the balancing force against the resultant of F₁ and F₂.Conclusion: Correct

This option suggests equal angles for a vector with equal magnitude components, such as î + ĵ + k̂, which is not applicable here.
This is the correct answer, as these angles are calculated using the direction cosines for the vector î + ĵ + √2k̂.
This option incorrectly assumes the largest angle is with the Z-axis, which does not align with the magnitude of its component √2.

questions.explanation: To determine the angles a vector makes with the coordinate axes, we use the direction cosines. For a vector v = aî + bĵ + ck̂, the cosine of the angle θx with the X-axis is cos(θx) = a/|v|, the cosine of the angle θy with the Y-axis is cos(θy) = b/|v|, and the cosine of the angle θz with the Z-axis is cos(θz) = c/|v|. Here, the vector is î + ĵ + √2k̂, hence a = 1, b = 1, and c = √2. The magnitude |v| is √(1² + 1² + (√2)²) = 2. Therefore, we have: cos(θx) = 1/2 leading to θx = 60°, cos(θy) = 1/2 leading to θy = 60°, cos(θz) = √2/2 leading to θz = 45°. Thus, the angles are 60°, 60°, and 45° respectively. Options A and B are incorrect as they suggest equal angles, which do not match the vector's components. Option D incorrectly attributes the largest angle to the Z-axis, which does not match the vector's magnitude distribution.

questions.explanation: The magnitude of the resultant force in vector addition is given by the formula: R = √(A² + B² + 2ABcosθ), where A and B are the magnitudes of the two forces and θ is the angle between them. As the angle θ increases from 0 to 180 degrees, the cosine of the angle decreases from 1 to -1, thus reducing the value of the resultant force R. Therefore, the magnitude of the resultant decreases as the angle increases.

questions.explanation: To solve this question, consider the triangle of forces. Let the smaller force have magnitude F and the larger one have magnitude 2F. Since the resultant is perpendicular to the smaller force, we can use the cosine rule or vector resolution to find the angle between the forces. The cosine of the angle θ between the vectors can be determined by setting the dot product of the resultant and the smaller vector to zero, yielding cos(θ) = -1/2. This corresponds to an angle of 120 degrees. Therefore, the correct answer is 1200. Other angles like 600, 1500, and 900 do not satisfy these conditions.

questions.text: The angle between two vectors of magnitude 12 and 18 units when their resultant is 24 units, will be:
questions.subject: Physics

questions.explanation: To find the angle between the two vectors, we can use the formula for the resultant of two vectors: R2 = A2 + B2 + 2ABcosθ Here, R is the resultant vector (24 units), and A and B are the magnitudes of the vectors (12 and 18 units respectively). We rearrange the formula to solve for the angle θ: cosθ = (R2 - A2 - B2) / (2AB) Substituting the values, we get: cosθ = (242 - 122 - 182) / (2 * 12 * 18) cosθ = (576 - 144 - 324) / 432 cosθ = 0.258 θ = cos-1(0.258) = 75.52° Thus, the correct answer is 75.52°. The other options either overestimate or underestimate the angle required to achieve a resultant of 24 units with the given vector magnitudes.

The distance is given as 7. Set up the equation: √((5 + 1)2 + (4 - 2)2 + (a + 2)2) = 7. Solving this gives (6)2 + (2)2 + (a + 2)2 = 49. Simplifying, 36 + 4 + (a + 2)2 = 49. questions.explanation: To solve this problem, first note that the greatest and least resultant forces given (10 N and 6 N) suggest the vectors are at angles of 0° and 180° respectively. Torque cannot be negative when the moment arm is zero because no force is applied over a distance.

This is incorrect because a non-zero torque requires a non-zero moment arm.

questions.explanation: The torque (τ) is calculated by the formula τ = r × F, where r is the moment arm and F is the force applied. If the moment arm (r) is zero, then the torque will also be zero, as no rotational effect can be produced regardless of the force applied. Thus, the correct answer is that τ = 0.Option A is incorrect because a positive value of torque greater than zero requires a non-zero moment arm. Option B is incorrect because torque cannot be negative in this context—it is either zero or positive depending on the force and moment arm. questions.text: A body is in equilibrium if the net force on it is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A net force of –1 N would cause acceleration, not equilibrium.
A net force of 0 N indicates that all forces are balanced, which is the condition for equilibrium.
A net force of 1⁄2 N would result in acceleration, disrupting equilibrium.
A net force of 1 N suggests unbalanced forces, causing acceleration.

questions.explanation: An object is in equilibrium when the net force acting upon it is 0 N. This means that all the forces cancel each other out, resulting in no acceleration of the object. Option B is correct because a net force of 0 N signifies balanced forces, a requirement for equilibrium. Option A is incorrect as a force of –1 N would lead to a net acceleration in the negative direction. Option C is incorrect because a force of 1⁄2 N would also lead to acceleration, disrupting equilibrium. Lastly, Option D is incorrect because a net force of 1 N indicates the presence of an unbalanced force, leading to motion rather than equilibrium.

questions.text: If the scalar product of two unit vectors is one (i-i= 1), then both vectors will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Parallel unit vectors have a dot product of 1 if they point in the same direction, as the angle between them is 0 degrees.
If the vectors could be in any direction, the dot product would not necessarily be 1, as the angle between them could vary.
Antiparallel unit vectors have a dot product of -1 because the angle between them is 180 degrees.
Perpendicular vectors have a dot product of 0 because the angle between them is 90 degrees.

questions.explanation: The scalar product or dot product of two vectors is given by a · b = |a||b|cosθ, where θ is the angle between the vectors. If both vectors are unit vectors, |a| = |b| = 1. Therefore, the dot product simplifies to cosθ = 1, which implies θ = 0 degrees. This means the vectors are parallel and point in the same direction. Option A is correct. Option B is incorrect because vectors in any direction do not guarantee a dot product of 1. Option C is incorrect because antiparallel vectors have a dot product of -1. Option D is incorrect because perpendicular vectors have a dot product of 0.

questions.text: If the components AX and Ay of vector A are negative and positive respectively, then vector A lies in
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In the I quadrant, both the x and y components of a vector are positive.
In the II quadrant, the x component is negative, and the y component is positive.
In the III quadrant, both the x and y components of a vector are negative.
In the IV quadrant, the x component is positive, and the y component is negative.

questions.explanation: The Cartesian plane is divided into four quadrants. In the II quadrant, vectors have a negative x-component and a positive y-component. Since vector A has AX negative and Ay positive, it lies in the II quadrant. Option A is incorrect because both components are positive in the I quadrant. Option C is incorrect as both components are negative in the III quadrant. Option D is incorrect because the x-component is positive and the y-component is negative in the IV quadrant.

questions.text: If the dot product of two vectors (X and Y) is zero, then which of the following conditions is TRUE?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Both vectors are opposite in direction
options.explanation: This is incorrect because vectors being opposite in direction would not necessarily result in a zero dot product.

options.text: Both vectors are parallel to each other
options.explanation: This is incorrect because parallel vectors will have a non-zero dot product unless one is a zero vector.

options.text: The vectors are orthogonal to each other
options.explanation: This is correct because a zero dot product indicates that the vectors are perpendicular, or orthogonal, to each other.

options.text: Either of the two vectors is a null vector
options.explanation: This is incorrect. While having a null vector would result in a zero dot product, it's not a definitive condition given in the question. Orthogonality is the specific condition implied here.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the vectors are orthogonal to each other. The dot product of two vectors is zero when the angle between them is 90 degrees, meaning they are perpendicular. Option A is incorrect because opposite vectors would have a negative dot product if they are not zero vectors. Option B is incorrect because parallel vectors (when not zero) have a non-zero dot product. Option D is incorrect because while a null vector would result in a zero dot product with any other vector, the question context suggests orthogonality.

questions.text: The cross product of two vectors (X and Y) is a negative vector when the angle between them is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: At 0o, the vectors are parallel and the cross product is zero, not negative.
At 90o, the cross product is maximized and perpendicular to the plane of the vectors, not negative.
At 180o, the vectors are anti-parallel, and the cross product is zero, not negative.
At 270o, the cross product is in the opposite direction compared to 90o, yielding a vector that is negative compared to the convention used for 90o.

questions.explanation: The cross product of two vectors results in a vector that is perpendicular to the plane containing the two vectors. The direction of the cross product vector is determined by the right-hand rule. At 270o, the cross product is effectively a negative version of the vector obtained at 90o, hence the result is considered negative. The angle of 0o and 180o results in zero cross product because the vectors are parallel or anti-parallel. At 90o, the cross product is at its maximum magnitude but not negative.

questions.text: Torque has minimum value when the angle between force F and force arm r is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: At 0o, the force is applied parallel to the force arm, resulting in no rotational effect and minimum torque.
At 30o, the force has a component that contributes to torque, but it is not at its maximum or minimum.
At 60o, the torque is greater than at 30o, but still not at its maximum.
At 90o, the force is perpendicular to the force arm, resulting in maximum torque.

questions.explanation: The torque is given by the formula τ = rF sin(θ), where τ is the torque, r is the lever arm, F is the force, and θ is the angle between the force and the lever arm. Torque has a minimum value when the angle θ is 0o because the sine of 0o is zero, resulting in zero torque. This means the force is applied in line with the lever arm, generating no rotation. The other angles, 30o, 60o, and 90o, result in greater values of torque as the sine of these angles is greater than zero, with 90o yielding the maximum torque.

questions.text: In a Cartesian plane, the x-axis is called
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The z-axis is typically used in three-dimensional coordinate systems, not in a standard two-dimensional Cartesian plane.

The x-axis is referred to as the horizontal axis in a Cartesian coordinate system.

questions.explanation: In a Cartesian plane, the x-axis is known as the horizontal axis because it runs horizontally from left to right. The y-axis, on the other hand, is the vertical axis. The z-axis is used in three-dimensional coordinate systems, and the term 'rotational axis' is not applicable in this context.

questions.text: If the value of moment arm is zero, then the value of torque will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Torque cannot be between 1 and 0 when the moment arm is zero because the torque is proportional to the moment arm.
Torque is a product of force and moment arm, which cannot be negative if both are zero or positive.
When the moment arm is zero, the torque must also be zero, as there is no distance to create a rotational effect.
Torque cannot be a constant value like 1 when the moment arm is zero, as torque depends on the moment arm.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is τ = 0. Torque (τ) is calculated as the product of the force applied and the moment arm (distance from the pivot point to the line of action of the force). If the moment arm is zero, no rotational effect can be produced, resulting in zero torque.Option A (1 > τ > 0) is incorrect because it suggests a non-zero torque, which is impossible with a zero moment arm. Option B (τ < 0) is incorrect because torque cannot be negative with a zero or positive moment arm. Option D (τ = 1) is incorrect because torque cannot arbitrarily be 1 without a moment arm.

questions.text: If the magnitude of a vector Y is the same as that of vector X but opposite in direction, then vector Y is equal to
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This suggests that vector Y is a unit vector, which is incorrect as the question focuses on direction rather than magnitude alone.
A vector with magnitude zero is a zero vector and does not apply here, as the question is about vectors with the same non-zero magnitude in opposite directions.
If Y were equal to X, they would have the same direction and magnitude, contradicting the 'opposite in direction' condition.
This means vector Y has the same magnitude as X but is in the opposite direction, accurately fulfilling the condition described in the question.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Y = -X. A vector's direction is indicated by its sign. If vector Y has the same magnitude as vector X but is opposite in direction, it means that Y is the negative of X. Hence, Y = -X. The other options are incorrect because they either ignore the directional component or misinterpret the meaning of opposite direction.

questions.text: According to the head to tail rule, if the forces are unequal in terms of direction and magnitude, then the minimum number of forces with the sum of zero will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Two unequal forces cannot sum to zero as they will not form a closed loop according to the head-to-tail rule.
Three forces can form a closed triangle, satisfying the condition for the sum of zero according to the head-to-tail rule.
Four or more forces can also sum to zero, but three is the minimum number required.
While five forces could sum to zero, it is not the minimum number required.

questions.explanation: The head-to-tail rule for vector addition states that vectors can be added by placing them head to tail, and the resultant vector is drawn from the tail of the first vector to the head of the last vector. For the vector sum to be zero, the vectors must form a closed loop. Three vectors can form a closed triangle, which is the simplest shape that satisfies this condition. Therefore, the minimum number of forces with unequal magnitude and direction that can sum to zero is three. Options A, C, and D suggest numbers of forces that are either too few to form a closed loop (in the case of two) or more than the minimum required (in the case of four and five).

This implies a larger net torque, indicating a greater rotational effect, which is not expected for a stable bridge.

questions.explanation: The correct answer isτ = 0. For a bridge anchored on two concrete pillars, it is expected to be in static equilibrium, meaning the net torque acting on it should be zero. This ensures that the bridge does not rotate and remains stable. The other options suggest that there is a net torque acting on the bridge, which would result in rotation and instability, which is not the case for a properly anchored and balanced bridge.

questions.text: If an object satisfies the first condition of equilibrium, then the rightward and leftward forces will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Equal in magnitude but opposite in direction
options.explanation: According to the first condition of equilibrium, also known as the condition for translational equilibrium, the sum of all horizontal forces must be zero. This means the rightward and leftward forces must cancel each other out by being equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.
If the forces were unequal in magnitude, the object would not be in equilibrium as there would be a net force causing motion.
Equilibrium cannot occur if there are only forces in one direction; forces must counteract each other for equilibrium.
Similar to the previous option, equilibrium requires forces in opposite directions to balance out.

questions.explanation: The first condition of equilibrium, also known as translational equilibrium, states that the sum of all forces acting on an object must be zero for it to be in equilibrium. This implies that all forces acting in the horizontal direction (rightward and leftward) must be equal in magnitude and opposite in direction. Therefore, the correct option is that the rightward and leftward forces are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction. The other options are incorrect because they do not satisfy this condition of equilibrium.

Option B is incorrect because the II quadrant has a negative x coordinate and a positive y coordinate. questions.subject: Physics

questions.text: The dot product of two perpendicular unit vectors is equal to
questions.subject: Physics

questions.text: In the given vector diagram, the point P is represented by the vector r.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A unit vector has a magnitude of one and is typically used to indicate direction. It does not describe a point in space.
A null vector has a magnitude of zero and represents no direction. It cannot represent a specific point in space.
Equal vectors have the same magnitude and direction but do not specify a point unless compared to another vector.
A position vector specifies the position of a point in space relative to the origin, making it the correct choice for representing point P.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is a position vector. A position vector is used to describe the position of a point in a coordinate system relative to a reference point, usually the origin. In this context, vector r represents the position of point P, making it a position vector. Other options like unit vector, null vector, and equal vector describe different properties of vectors but do not specifically refer to the position of a point in space.

questions.text: The given figure depicts one of the conditions of equilibrium.Which of the following options identifies the CORRECT name and statement of the condition?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: First - The Vector sum of all the torques is zero
options.explanation: A) First - The Vector sum of all the torques is zero:This statement correctly describes the second condition of equilibrium, but it's incorrectly labeled as "First."

options.text: Second - The Vector sum of all the forces is zero
options.explanation: B) Second - The Vector sum of all the forces is zero:This statement correctly describes the first condition of equilibrium, but it's incorrectly labeled as "Second."

options.text: First - The Vector sum of all the momenta is zero
options.explanation: C) First - The Vector sum of all the momenta is zero:This statement is incorrect as momentum is related to motion, not directly to the static conditions of equilibrium.

options.text: Second - The Vector sum of all the torques is zero
options.explanation: D) Second - The Vector sum of all the torques is zero:This option correctly identifies the "Second" condition of equilibrium and accurately states that "The Vector sum of all the torques is zero," which is the condition for rotational equilibrium.

questions.explanation: First Condition of Equilibrium:The vector sum of all forces acting on an object is zero. This ensures translational equilibrium (no acceleration in any direction).Second Condition of Equilibrium:The vector sum of all torques acting on an object is zero. This ensures rotational equilibrium (no angular acceleration).
